title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Quick Start

Course Schedule - Weekly schedule and course materials
Course Syllabus - Detailed course information and policies
Lectures - All course lectures organized by week
Assessments - Quizzes, exams, and projects
Resources - Textbook, R downloads, and reference materials

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Overview
STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics (4 units) introduces descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, distributions, confidence intervals, hypothesis testing, regression, and ANOVA with emphasis on applications in biological and natural resource sciences.
Lecture: Monday, Wednesday, Friday, 11:00–11:50 AM, BSS 166
Lab: Thursday (select one section: 9:00–10:50 AM, 11:00–12:50 PM, or 1:00–2:50 PM), FH 202
General Education: Lower Division GE Area 2 (Quantitative Reasoning)

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Course Structure
Includes collaborative daily warm-ups, weekly programming labs, weekly quizzes, two midterms, and five portfolio ready projects. Optional final exam may replace one midterm grade.

title: STAT 109: Introductory Biostatistics
text:
Instructor Information
Instructor: Dr. Rosanna Overholser
Office: BSS 334
Email: rho3@humboldt.edu
Office Hours: Mondays 2-3pm, Wednesdays 12-12:50pm, Fridays 10-10:50am, or by appointment

title: Quiz 8
text:
These practice problems cover Lab 7 and Lectures 18-21:

confidence intervals for one mean and one proportion,
when to use FPC,
checking interval conditions,
convenience vs simple random sampling (SRS),
sampling frame and bias,
interpreting confidence intervals in context.











NoteQuick reference for Quiz 8





\[
\bar{x} \pm t^* \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
\]

Statistic: \(\bar{x}\)

SE: \(s/\sqrt{n}\)

Conditions: random sample (or SRS), and population roughly normal or \(n\) moderately large




\[
\bar{x} \pm t^* \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}} \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
\]

Use when population is finite and \(n/N\) is not small.




\[
\hat{p} \pm z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}}
\]

Statistic: \(\hat{p}=x/n\)

SE: \(\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})/n}\)

Conditions: SRS/random sample, independent outcomes, and large counts (\(n\hat{p} \ge 10\), \(n(1-\hat{p}) \ge 10\))




\[
\hat{p} \pm z^* \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})}{n}} \sqrt{\frac{N-n}{N-1}}
\]

Use when sampling without replacement from known finite \(N\) and \(n/N\) is not small.









Work without notes first, then check your work.
Show setup and reasoning, not only final numbers.
Unless a problem says otherwise, use 95% confidence.







Define population in one or two clear sentences.




Define sample in one or two clear sentences.




Define parameter in one or two clear sentences.




Define statistic in one or two clear sentences.




Define sampling frame in one or two clear sentences.




Define bias in the context of sampling and estimation.




Define 95% confidence level in terms of repeated sampling and intervals.




Define confidence interval in one or two clear sentences.





For each scenario:

Identify the population.
Identify the sample.
Identify the parameter.
Identify the statistic (and compute it if enough info is given).




A random sample of \(n=120\) undergraduates is selected to estimate the proportion who work at least 10 hours/week. In the sample, \(x=46\) say yes.




A district has \(N=300\) classrooms. An SRS of \(n=30\) classrooms is taken to estimate average number of chairs. The sample mean is \(\bar{x}=29.8\) chairs.




A clinic wants the proportion of all current patients who are very satisfied. It samples \(n=80\) patients this month, and 52 report “very satisfied.”




A researcher wants the mean number of minutes/day spent on course websites by all STAT 109 students. She samples 25 students and computes \(\bar{x}=18.4\) minutes.





For each scenario:

Classify as convenience or SRS.
Is bias likely present? (Yes/No + one sentence).
State one sentence about whether results can reasonably generalize to the target population.




A student stands outside one dining hall from 12-1 p.m. and surveys the first 60 students who walk by about sleep habits.




From a full registrar list of all undergraduates (ID 1 to \(N\)), a random-number generator is used to select 120 unique IDs. Those students are surveyed.




A researcher surveys 40 students attending the weekly pre-med club meeting to estimate the campus proportion planning to apply to medical school.




An instructor creates a random sample from a list of students with declared majors only; undeclared students are not in the list.





List the SRS workflow in order using these three actions:

list population units (or frame),
generate random numbers,
collect data.

Then apply those steps to the setting below.


Goal: estimate mean weekly study hours for all STAT 109 students (\(N=210\)). Desired sample size: \(n=30\).
Write 3-4 bullets describing exactly how you would carry out the SRS.





For each scenario:

Is the parameter a mean or proportion?
Use FPC or no FPC?
Are the interval conditions reasonably met? (brief justification)




Sample of \(n=220\) students; \(x=82\) have a part-time job. The sample is SRS from a very large statewide population.




A district has \(N=300\) classrooms. SRS without replacement gives \(n=30\) classroom chair counts; you want a CI for average chairs/classroom.




From \(N=80\) patients at a clinic, \(n=50\) are sampled without replacement. Outcome is Yes/No (successful recovery), and you want a CI for the population success proportion.




SRS of \(n=18\) students from a large campus. Outcome is whether the student is a biology major; observed \(\hat{p}=2/18\).





For each item, write a correct interpretation sentence in context.


A 95% CI for the proportion of all undergraduates who are first-generation college students is \([0.22, 0.30]\).




A 95% CI for the mean number of minutes/day students spend on course websites is \([41, 55]\) minutes.




A school district sampled from a finite list of schools and computed a 95% CI (with FPC) for the proportion of schools older than 50 years: \([0.14, 0.27]\).




Choose the best interpretation of a 95% CI and explain briefly why:

A. “There is a 95% probability that the true parameter is in this specific interval.”
B. “If we repeatedly took samples the same way and built intervals this way, about 95% of those intervals would contain the true parameter.”
C. “95% of individuals in the population fall between the interval endpoints.”






For each problem, do not recompute by hand. Use the shown output.

Identify whether it is a CI for a mean or proportion.
Write the 95% CI from the output.
Interpret it in context in one sentence.



Code
# Example 1: proportion CI from a sample
n <- 220
x <- 80
prop.test(x, n, conf.level = 0.95, correct = FALSE)



    1-sample proportions test without continuity correction

data:  x out of n, null probability 0.5
X-squared = 16.364, df = 1, p-value = 5.228e-05
alternative hypothesis: true p is not equal to 0.5
95 percent confidence interval:
 0.3029152 0.4290379
sample estimates:
        p 
0.3636364 





Is this estimating a mean or a proportion?
What is the 95% interval (from the printed output)?
Write a context interpretation.




Code
# Example 2: mean CI from quantitative data
x <- c(12, 18, 25, 9, 31, 16, 20, 22, 14, 3, 19, 11, 24, 30, 15, 15, 21, 26, 13, 28, 10, 23, 29, 18, 33)
t.test(x, conf.level = 0.95)



    One Sample t-test

data:  x
t = 12.558, df = 24, p-value = 4.853e-12
alternative hypothesis: true mean is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 16.21152 22.58848
sample estimates:
mean of x 
     19.4 






Is this estimating a mean or a proportion?
What is the 95% interval (from the printed output)?
Write a context interpretation.


End of Quiz 8 practice problems.
